You're at your standing desk on Tuesday morning, shoulders pinned near your ears, trying to roll them down — they rise again before the standup starts because straight has started to feel wrong.

Your shoulders climb toward your ears before dawn. The catastrophizing arrives before your feet hit the floor—a sick spiral of worst-case scenarios about school, illness, car accidents, the climate. Y
You're on the nursery floor at 8:30 PM, toddler climbing your back, work phone buzzing in your pocket — you answer before you decide because saying no does not feel available tonight.
You're at the kitchen sink at 9:30 PM, dishes in your hands, shoulders sagging — the kids are down, the laptop is closed, and your body is still running the day it cannot finish.

You're loading the dishwasher at 9 PM, back aching, still wearing the smile you gave your child at homework time — you gave to your partner and your mother and your boss today and your chest is empty.
You're in the hallway at bedtime, child screaming on the floor, feet planted, jaw set — your body wants to rescue and you are standing in the discomfort because you know what rescue costs.
You're in the minivan pickup line, hands on the wheel, face neutral for the teacher — the flatness stayed after postpartum and five years later you are still performing fine.
You're at the table with the childcare invoice and the mortgage statement side by side, breath shallow — you work to pay for care so you can work and the math loops without landing.
You're in the home office when the daycare charge posts, shoulders rising — childcare costs more than the house payment this month and your thumb hesitates before you click approve.
You're in the bathroom mirror at 6 AM, toothbrush in hand, eyes on your own face — you are grieving the person you were before kids and the person with time is not coming back.
You're at the school conference table, folder open, smile on, palms damp — you are managing kids and a job and still waiting to be told you are failing at both.
You're on the playground bench watching your child climb, phone buzzing with work, mind on the email — you are in two places and your jaw is tight in both.
You're at the pediatrician's office filling out the behavior form, pen moving, chest tight — good kid equals good parent equals your value as a human and the equation is lying to you.
You're on the couch at 9:45 PM when the house finally goes quiet, shoulders dropping then locking — your brain takes the first silence of the day as the opening shift for tomorrow's worry.
You're at the birthday party in the community room, plate in hand, eyes flicking to your phone — your attention is split across three places and the room feels like one more demand on your chest.
You're sitting on the edge of the bathtub after the kids are down, shoulders still at your ears, rolling your neck once — the tension has been there so long you forgot your body was making a choice.
